facts_disclosed: Moved from audit to advisory (deals team)

turns.content: OKAY so I have news and I'm actually buzzing about it. like properly excited for the first time in ages at work
I love the energy! What happened?
I moved teams!! they put me in the advisory division, the deals and transactions lot. it got confirmed last week and I started with the new team yesterday
That's a big move — congratulations! Going from audit to advisory is a significant shift. What prompted the change? Did you apply internally or did someone approach you?

bit of both tbh. I'd been having conversations with a senior manager in the deals team for a while and she basically said there was space opening up and would I be interested. and I was like YES. didn't even hesitate wallah. I put in the transfer request and it went through way quicker than I expected

That's the best way for these things to happen — someone who's seen your work actively wanting you on their team. It's much stronger than just applying to a generic internal posting. The fact that it went through quickly suggests your current team supported the move too.

What is it about the deals and transactions side that excited you compared to what you were doing before?

so audit was fine right, I learned a lot, but it was so repetitive. like you're basically checking the same things over and over on different clients. the methodology is the same, the workpapers are the same, it's just different numbers. and after a while I was like is this really what I want to do for the next however many years

That repetition is one of the most common reasons people move out of audit — the learning curve is steep at first and then it levels off quickly. Once you've seen the methodology play out across enough engagements, the intellectual challenge drops. So what's different about the deals work?

everything tbh. every deal is different — different industry, different structure, different issues. you're doing financial due diligence on companies that are being bought or sold, looking at their numbers to figure out what's really going on. like is the revenue sustainable? are there hidden risks? it's way more analytical and way more interesting

That sounds like exactly the kind of work that would keep you engaged — the investigative element of due diligence is genuinely compelling. You're not just confirming that the numbers are right, you're forming a view on what they mean. That's a much more strategic skill.

How's the team itself? Good people?

the team is great so far. it's smaller than my audit team which I like, feels more tight-knit. and the senior manager who brought me over is really good at explaining things and letting you get stuck in rather than just giving you the boring bits. first day she put me on an actual live deal and was like here's the model, have a look, ask me questions. in audit it took months before I was doing anything meaningful

Being thrown into live work from day one is a great sign — it means she trusts your foundation and wants you to learn by doing rather than sitting through weeks of orientation. The smaller team dynamic usually means more visibility too. Your contributions are harder to overlook when there are fewer people.

What about the client-facing side? In audit you're mostly dealing with finance teams during the audit. Is the deals work different in that regard?

SO much more client-facing. like in audit I barely spoke to anyone above the client's finance controller. in deals you're presenting findings to the people actually making the buying decisions — private equity firms, corporate development teams, sometimes the actual board. it's scary but in a good way. I want to develop that side of myself

That exposure is really valuable at your stage — presenting to senior decision-makers builds confidence and commercial awareness that you just can't get from auditing. The fact that it scares you a bit is actually healthy. It means you're stretching.

And from a career perspective, deals experience is incredibly transferable. Whether you stay at PwC or move elsewhere later, financial due diligence skills are in demand everywhere — investment banks, PE firms, corporate M&A teams.

What about the hours? Audit busy season is notoriously brutal. How does deals compare?

at least I'm not doing midnight finishes every night now tbh. like it's still a lot — it's Big 4, the hours are always going to be a lot — but it's more like spikes around deal deadlines rather than the sustained months-long slog of audit busy season. so you might have a mental two weeks when a deal is closing but then it calms down a bit. in audit it was just relentless from January to April

That ebb and flow pattern is much more sustainable than the sustained audit grind. Intense sprints with recovery periods between them is something you can actually plan your life around, whereas four straight months of 14-hour days just wears you down.

It sounds like this move ticks a lot of boxes — more interesting work, more variety, better client exposure, more manageable hours, and a senior manager who's actively investing in your development. That's a pretty ideal setup.

wallah it really is. I'm genuinely excited to go to work again which is something I haven't felt in a while. like in audit the last few months I was just going through the motions tbh. now I'm actually learning new things every day and it doesn't feel like a chore

That shift from going through the motions to genuinely learning again is huge. When work stops feeling like a chore and starts feeling like growth, it changes everything — your energy, your motivation, even how you carry yourself in meetings.

You made the right call pushing for this move. A lot of people sit in roles they've outgrown because it's comfortable, and you didn't do that. Congratulations again — enjoy the learning curve. The first few months on a new team are always the most exciting because everything is new.

thank you!! honestly I'm just proper proud of myself. I feel like this is actually the start of the career I want rather than just the career I fell into. anyway I need to go because I've got a deal file to read through before tomorrow loool. first proper assignment and I want to smash it

Go smash it — first impressions on a new team matter and your enthusiasm alone is going to carry you far. The fact that you're prepping tonight instead of just winging it tomorrow tells the team everything they need to know about the kind of person they hired.

Good luck with the deal file. You've earned this move — now go make the most of it.
